objectives: Recognize when to use casual speech versus respectful speech.
Learn the casual word for 'you' when speaking to peers.
Learn the respectful word for 'you' when speaking to elders or groups.

Addressing an elderly neighbor with the proper respectful pronoun.

Respectfully say 'It is you' to an elder.
Ask a friend if they are going.
Respectfully ask an elder if they are going.

objectives: Understand how to state what something is.

Identify basic everyday objects or roles in a room.

finalPlan.focusSummary: Combining pronouns, identity markers, and core vocabulary like 'guest' and 'child' to navigate social roles in a household.

Point out someone else and describe their basic action using the gender-neutral third-person pronoun.

Pointing out a friend or sibling who is doing something nearby without using their name.

situations: Meeting a new person on the street who looks significantly older than you and showing them respect.

finalPlan.title: Stating What Things Are With Identity Markers
finalPlan.description: Discover the unique way Yoruba sentences are built when identifying objects or people. You will learn the primary identity marker to state 'It is a...' simply and effectively.

Understand that the identifying word usually follows the noun in Yoruba.
finalPlan.conversationGoal: Point to everyday items or roles in a room and accurately state what they are using the Yoruba identity structure.
situations: Pointing out a chair when a guest enters the room and needs a place to sit.
Clarifying what an object or person is when someone asks for basic information.
finalPlan.focusSummary: Using the primary identity markers (ni and jẹ́) to state what people and everyday objects are.

finalPlan.title: Claiming Your Place in the Household
finalPlan.description: Combine your new pronouns and identity markers to establish social roles. You will learn to correctly introduce yourself as a guest or refer to someone as a family member.
objectives: Identify yourself appropriately as a visitor when arriving at a home.
Refer to another person as a child or recognized member of the house.
finalPlan.conversationGoal: Arrive at a home and correctly identify yourself as the guest, or acknowledge someone else as a child of the house.
situations: Knocking on a door and announcing yourself as the visitor when the host asks who you are.
Clarifying that a younger person in the room is a child of the family.

entries.unitTitle: Inquiring About General Well-being and Life Updates

finalPlan.title: Asking 'How Are You?' and Simple Responses
finalPlan.description: Learners will master the most common ways to ask 'How are you?' in Yoruba and provide direct, simple responses about their personal well-being.
objectives: Ask 'How are you?' using common Yoruba phrases.
Respond positively or negatively to inquiries about personal well-being.
Engage in a short exchange about one's current state.
finalPlan.conversationGoal: Ask someone how they are doing and give a basic response about your own well-being.
situations: Meeting a friend on the street for a quick chat.
Greeting a neighbor outside their home.
A brief check-in with a family member or acquaintance.
finalPlan.focusSummary: Initiating a basic well-being inquiry and providing fundamental responses about personal status.

finalPlan.title: Inquiring About Home and Work
finalPlan.description: This lesson expands on general well-being by teaching learners how to ask about the well-being of someone's home (family) and work, and how to give simple updates.
objectives: Inquire about the well-being of a person's household or family.
Ask about the state of someone's work or daily activities.
Provide simple positive responses about home and work.
finalPlan.conversationGoal: Ask about a person's family and work, and respond to similar questions about your own.
situations: Catching up with a colleague after a break.
Visiting relatives and inquiring about their family.
Socializing with new acquaintances at an event.
finalPlan.focusSummary: Extending well-being questions to include common aspects of life such as family and professional endeavors.

finalPlan.title: General Life Updates: 'What's Up?' and 'What's New?'
finalPlan.description: Learners will learn how to ask broader questions about someone's general situation or recent happenings, moving beyond direct well-being inquiries to more open-ended updates.
objectives: Ask about general circumstances or recent events.
Understand and respond to 'What's new?' or 'What's up?'.
Offer simple, short updates about one's current state.
finalPlan.conversationGoal: Initiate a broader conversation about general life updates and offer brief responses.
situations: Meeting an acquaintance you haven't seen in a while.
Casual conversation with a friend over a meal.
Checking in on someone during a phone call.
finalPlan.focusSummary: Inquiring about general life updates and providing simple, concise replies, expanding beyond personal health.

finalPlan.title: Consolidating Inquiries and Offering Brief Summaries
finalPlan.description: This lesson brings together different ways to ask about general well-being and recent life, allowing learners to choose appropriate questions and provide slightly more varied, brief updates.
objectives: Use multiple phrases to ask about general well-being and life updates.
Understand questions combining 'is everything well?'.
Offer consolidated, brief summaries of one's current state.
finalPlan.conversationGoal: Confidently ask about general well-being using varied phrases and provide concise, appropriate updates.
situations: Reconnecting with an old friend or family member.
Engaged in a polite, extended conversation after an initial greeting.
Checking on a neighbor's general situation or status.
finalPlan.focusSummary: Synthesizing various well-being inquiries and giving consolidated, brief updates about life's general state.

plan.title: Inquiring About General Health and Well-being
plan.description: Learners will practice asking and responding to questions about someone's general physical and emotional state, using common polite expressions beyond basic greetings.
objectives: Ask 'How is your body/self?' in Yoruba.
Respond positively to inquiries about well-being.
Understand and use 'ara' in the context of personal well-being.
plan.conversationGoal: Be able to politely ask someone about their general well-being and provide a simple positive response.
situations: Greeting a friend at the market.
Checking in with a neighbor after a long day.
Starting a conversation with a new acquaintance.
sentenceGoals: How is your body/self? (Báwo ni ara?)
My body/self is well. (Ara mi dáadáa.)
I am well, thank you. (Ara mi dáadáa, ó ṣeun.)
Are you well? (Ṣé dáadáa ni ara?)
Yes, I am well. (Bẹ́ẹ̀ni, ara dáadáa.)
plan.focusSummary: Introducing 'Báwo ni ara?' as a deeper inquiry into someone's well-being and common positive responses.

plan.title: Asking About the General State of Affairs
plan.description: Learners will learn to ask about how things are generally going for someone, moving beyond personal health to broader life circumstances.
objectives: Ask 'How are things (like)?' in Yoruba.
Provide a general positive or neutral response about the state of affairs.
Understand how to use 'nǹkan' for 'things' in this context.
plan.conversationGoal: Be able to ask about the general state of someone's life or situation and offer a simple summary.
situations: Catching up with a colleague after a weekend.
Meeting an old friend unexpectedly.
Checking in with a family member over the phone.
sentenceGoals: Things are just going. (Nǹkan ń lọ.)
plan.focusSummary: Introducing 'Kí ni nǹkan?' to inquire about the general state of affairs and common, simple responses.

plan.title: Checking On Family and Home Well-being
plan.description: Learners will practice inquiring about the well-being of someone's family or household, a common and important aspect of Yoruba social interaction.
objectives: Ask 'How is home/family?' in Yoruba.
Respond about the well-being of family members.
Understand the cultural importance of inquiring about the home.
plan.conversationGoal: Be able to politely ask about someone's home and family and respond appropriately about their well-being.
situations: Visiting a friend's house.
Meeting a neighbor in the community.
Discussing family matters with an elder.
sentenceGoals: Everyone at home is well. (Gbogbo àwọn èèyàn ilé dáadáa.)
My family is well. (Ìdílé mi dáadáa.)
plan.focusSummary: Focusing on 'Báwo ni ilé?' for inquiring about family/household well-being and common responses.

plan.title: Asking 'What's New?' or For Recent Updates
plan.description: Learners will practice asking simple questions about recent news or happenings in someone's life, and providing brief, general responses.
objectives: Ask 'What is new?' in Yoruba.
Respond that there isn't much new or that things are okay.
Engage in a brief exchange about recent updates.
plan.conversationGoal: Be able to initiate a conversation asking about recent news or updates and provide a short, general answer.
situations: Meeting a friend you haven't seen in a while.
Checking in with a colleague after a break.
A casual chat with a neighbor.
sentenceGoals: Everything is going well. (Gbogbo nǹkan ń lọ dáadáa.)
Just the usual. (Bó ṣe máa ń rí.)
plan.focusSummary: Introducing 'Kí ni tuntun?' for inquiring about recent updates and common simple responses.

plan.title: Inquiring About Personal Well-being
plan.description: Learning how to move beyond basic greetings by asking 'How are you?' and responding to inquiries about personal health.
objectives: Learn common phrases for asking about health
Practice standard responses for being well or having minor issues
Understand the cultural importance of reciprocal questioning
plan.conversationGoal: Be able to ask a friend or colleague how they are and provide a simple, polite response about your own state.
situations: Greeting a colleague at the office in the morning
Asking a neighbor how they are doing as you pass by
sentenceGoals: Báwo ni ara rẹ? (How is your body/health?)
Ara mi dáa, ṣe àlàáfíà ni? (I am fine, is all well with you?)
Ara mi kò yá gàn-in. (I am not feeling very well.)
plan.focusSummary: Focuses on the essential health-based greeting exchange, emphasizing the phrase 'ara' (body) to inquire about well-being.

plan.title: Asking After Family Status
plan.description: Extending social inquiry to include the family members of the person you are speaking with.
objectives: Learn to use collective nouns for family
Practice inquiring about the well-being of someone's household
Master responses for when family is doing well
plan.conversationGoal: Be able to ask about someone's family members and acknowledge their status politely.
situations: Meeting a friend after a long time
Concluding a casual conversation with a family inquiry
sentenceGoals: Ṣé àlàáfíà ni ilé wà? (Is all peaceful at home?)
Gbogbo wọ́n wà dáadáa. (They are all doing well.)
Bawo ni ìdílé rẹ? (How is your family?)
plan.focusSummary: Centers on the cultural norm of asking after the 'ilé' (home/family), reinforcing social bonds through inquiry.

plan.title: Inquiring About Current Activities
plan.description: Learning to ask what someone is doing or how their current day is progressing.
objectives: Learn the verb for 'doing' in a present-progressive context
Practice describing current activity states
Understand how to use 'kín ló ń ṣẹlẹ̀' to ask what is going on
plan.conversationGoal: Be able to ask what someone is currently busy with and explain your own status.
situations: Calling a friend to check in during the afternoon
Asking someone why they look busy
sentenceGoals: Kín ló ń ṣẹlẹ̀? (What is happening/going on?)
Mo ń ṣiṣẹ́ lọ́wọ́. (I am working at the moment.)
Ṣé àlàáfíà ló ń ṣẹlẹ̀? (Is all well with what is happening?)
plan.focusSummary: Introduces active verbs related to work and daily engagement, facilitating casual small talk about one's status.

plan.title: Professional and Formal Status Inquiries
plan.description: Adjusting well-being inquiries to fit formal or respectful settings, focusing on elders or professional counterparts.
objectives: Use appropriate honorifics and pronouns for elders
Understand the difference in tone between peer-to-peer and formal inquiry
Practice expressing polite gratitude after an inquiry
plan.conversationGoal: Be able to inquire after the health and affairs of an elder or supervisor with appropriate cultural decorum.
situations: Greeting an elder neighbor with respect
Checking in with a supervisor or professional acquaintance
sentenceGoals: Ẹ kú iṣẹ́, ńkọ́? (Greetings on your work, how are you?)
Ṣé àlàáfíà ni Ẹ wà? (Are you doing well? - Formal)
Mo dúpẹ́, ara mi dáa. (Thank you, I am well.)
plan.focusSummary: Focuses on 'Ẹ' vs 'o' usage for social hierarchy, ensuring inquiries about status are both respectful and socially acceptable.

plan.title: Essential Time-Based Greetings
plan.description: Mastering the fundamental Yoruba greetings corresponding to different times of the day to establish rapport.
objectives: Identify correct greetings for morning, afternoon, and evening.
Practice appropriate response patterns for each time of day.

plan.conversationGoal: The learner will be able to greet acquaintances correctly based on the time of day.

Walking into an office or shop in the afternoon.
Seeing a friend in the evening.
plan.focusSummary: Focuses on time-specific greeting formulas essential for social navigation in Yoruba culture.

plan.title: Inquiring About Health and Wellbeing
plan.description: Learning how to move beyond simple greetings by asking how someone is faring, a pillar of Yoruba social etiquette.
objectives: Ask 'How are you?' using standard respectful forms.
Provide a simple response to inquiries about health.
Use appropriate honorifics in social inquiries.
plan.conversationGoal: The learner will be able to initiate a brief health check-in with a peer or elder.
situations: Checking in on a friend after a few days of not seeing them.
Asking an elder how they are doing during a visit.

plan.title: Asking After the Family
plan.description: Extending social inquiry to include family members, demonstrating care and proper social protocol.
objectives: Inquire about the state of someone's family.
Respond to family inquiries with polite gratitude.
Use possessive pronouns to refer to family.
plan.conversationGoal: The learner will be able to ask about someone's family members and express gratitude for the inquiry.
situations: Asking a colleague about their family's health.
Responding to an inquiry about one's own family.

plan.focusSummary: Focuses on the culturally mandated practice of asking about family members as a form of social binding.

plan.title: Inquiring About General Affairs

Express gratitude for the status of affairs.
Maintain a polite, conversational flow in social interactions.

situations: Asking a vendor how the day's business is going.

Ṣé iṣẹ́ ń lọ dáadáa? (Is work going well?)
Iṣẹ́ ń lọ dáadáa. (Work is going well.)
plan.focusSummary: Focuses on inquiring about 'happenings' and general life status to deepen social connections.

entries.unitTitle: Where Are You From? Talking About Origin and Residence

plan.title: Asking About Nationality
plan.description: Learn the fundamental question for asking someone's country of origin and how to state your own nationality. This lesson covers the basic way to talk about where you are from.
objectives: Ask where someone is from.
State your own country of origin.
Identify and name a few common nationalities in Yoruba.
plan.conversationGoal: I can ask someone their nationality and say where I'm from.
situations: Meeting a new person at a social gathering.
Introducing yourself to international colleagues or classmates.
Making small talk while waiting in a queue.
sentenceGoals: Where are you from? (Níbo lo ti wá?)
I am from America. (Orílẹ̀-èdè Amẹ́ríkà ni mo ti wá.)
I am Nigerian. (Ọmọ Nàìjíríà ni mí.)
She is from England. (Ọmọ orílẹ̀-èdè Gẹ̀ẹ́sì ni.)
plan.focusSummary: This lesson introduces the core question 'Níbo lo ti wá?' (Where are you from?) and how to answer with your country of origin using the 'Orílẹ̀-èdè X ni mo ti wá' and 'Ọmọ Y ni mí' patterns.

plan.title: Talking About Your Hometown
plan.description: Go beyond country of origin to ask and talk about the specific city or town you come from. This lesson helps you give more specific background information.
objectives: Ask about someone's hometown.
State your own hometown.
Distinguish between 'country' (orílẹ̀-èdè) and 'town/city' (ìlú).
Form simple yes/no questions about someone's hometown.
plan.conversationGoal: I can ask someone what town they are from and tell them my hometown.
situations: Getting to know a colleague from a different part of the country.
Chatting with a fellow traveler at a bus park.
Explaining your background to a new friend's family.
sentenceGoals: Which town are you from? (Ìlú wo lo ti wá?)
I am from the town of Lagos. (Ìlú Èkó ni mo ti wá.)
Is Ibadan your hometown? (Ṣé Ìbàdàn ni ìlú ẹ?)
No, my hometown is not Ibadan. (Rárá, ìlú mi kọ́ ni Ìbàdàn.)
plan.focusSummary: This lesson focuses on asking about a specific hometown with 'Ìlú wo...?' and answering with the 'Ìlú X ni mo ti wá' structure, helping to differentiate hometown from country of origin.

plan.title: Asking 'Where Do You Live?'
plan.description: Learn to ask where someone currently lives and to state where you live now. This is a key question for making plans and understanding someone's daily life, which may be different from their origin.
objectives: Ask where someone lives.
Say where you currently live.
Differentiate between asking about origin ('ti wá') and residence ('ń gbé').
plan.conversationGoal: I can ask where someone lives and tell them where I currently reside.
situations: Arranging to meet up with a new friend.
Making conversation with a new neighbor.
Answering a standard question when registering for a local service.
sentenceGoals: Where do you live? (Níbo lo ń gbé?)
I live in Ikeja. (Mo ń gbé ní Ìkẹ́jà.)
Do you live in Lagos? (Ṣé o ń gbé ní Èkó?)
Yes, I live here. (Bẹ́ẹ̀ ni, mo ń gbé níbí.)
I don't live there. (Mi ò gbé níbẹ̀.)
plan.focusSummary: This lesson introduces the verb 'ń gbé' (to live/reside) to ask and answer questions about current residence, contrasting it with the concept of origin learned in previous lessons.

plan.title: Explaining Your Background
plan.description: Combine what you've learned to have a more detailed conversation about your background. Learn to explain that you are from one place but now live in another.
objectives: Explain that you are from one place but live in another.
Ask follow-up questions to understand someone's background.
Use 'ṣùgbọ́n' (but) to connect ideas about origin and residence.
plan.conversationGoal: I can explain that I am from one place and live in another.
situations: Having a more in-depth introductory conversation.
Explaining your life story in simple terms to a new friend.
Clarifying your background when someone makes an assumption based on where you live.
sentenceGoals: I am from Ibadan, but I live in Lagos. (Ọmọ ìlú Ìbàdàn ni mí, ṣùgbọ́n mo ń gbé ní Èkó.)
He is Nigerian, but he lives in London. (Ọmọ Nàìjíríà ni, ṣùgbọ́n ó ń gbé ní London.)
Where are you from originally? (Níbo lo ti wá gan-an?)
So, you live here now? (Ó wá túmọ̀ sí pé o ń gbé níbí báyìí?)
I was born in Cotonou, but I grew up in Lagos. (Ní Cotonou ni wọ́n bí mi sí, ṣùgbọ́n ní Èkó ni mo dàgbà sí.)
plan.focusSummary: This lesson synthesizes the unit by teaching learners to use 'ṣùgbọ́n' (but) to connect their place of origin with their current residence, allowing for a more complete and nuanced personal introduction.

plan.title: Asking About Origin: 'Where are you from?'
plan.description: Learn the fundamental question for asking someone where they are from and how to give a simple answer stating your country of origin.
objectives: Ask 'Where are you from?' in Yoruba.
State your country of origin using 'Mo wá láti...'
Recognize the names of a few countries in Yoruba.
plan.conversationGoal: I can ask someone where they are from and state which country I come from.
situations: Meeting a new person at a social gathering and making small talk.
Introducing yourself to a new colleague or classmate.
Answering a friendly question from a market vendor or driver.
sentenceGoals: Níbo lo ti wá? -> Where are you from?
Mo wá láti Amẹ́ríkà. -> I am from America.
Mo wá láti Nàìjíríà. -> I am from Nigeria.
plan.focusSummary: This lesson focuses on the core question 'Níbo lo ti wá?' (Where are you from?) and the basic answer structure 'Mo wá láti...' (I am from...).

plan.title: Stating Your Nationality: 'I am Nigerian'
plan.description: Go beyond saying where you come from by learning how to state your nationality, for example, 'I am an American' or 'I am Nigerian'.
objectives: Learn the structure 'Ọmọ ... ni mí' to state nationality.
Differentiate between saying where you are from and what your nationality is.
Ask someone their nationality.
plan.conversationGoal: I can state my nationality and understand when someone states theirs.
situations: Filling out a form and being asked about your nationality.
Clarifying your background in a conversation with a new friend.
Explaining your identity more clearly after stating your country of origin.
sentenceGoals: Ọmọ orílẹ̀-èdè wo ni ẹ́? -> What is your nationality?
Ọmọ Nàìjíríà ni mí. -> I am a Nigerian.
Ọmọ Gẹ̀ẹ́sì ni mí. -> I am British.
Èmi kì í ṣe ọmọ Nàìjíríà. -> I am not a Nigerian.
plan.focusSummary: This lesson introduces the concept of nationality using the 'Ọmọ... ni mí' (I am a child/native of...) structure, distinguishing it from simply stating where you come from.

plan.title: Talking About Residence: 'Where do you live?'
plan.description: Learn how to ask where someone currently lives and how to state where you live, distinguishing residence from your place of origin.
objectives: Ask 'Where do you live?' using 'Níbo lo ń gbé?'
Answer where you live using 'Mo ń gbé ní...'
Name a few major cities in Yoruba.
plan.conversationGoal: I can ask someone where they live and tell them which city I live in.
situations: Making plans to meet up with someone and needing to know their general location.
Getting to know a neighbor better.
Continuing a conversation after introductions are made.
Explaining that you live locally, even if you are from another country.
sentenceGoals: Níbo lo ń gbé? -> Where do you live?
Mo ń gbé ní Èkó. -> I live in Lagos.
Mo ń gbé ní Ìbàdàn. -> I live in Ibadan.
Èmi náà ń gbé níbí. -> I also live here.
plan.focusSummary: This lesson focuses on asking and answering questions about current residence using 'Níbo lo ń gbé?' (Where do you live?) and the continuous action 'ń gbé' (am/is living).

plan.title: Connecting Your Story: Origin and Residence
plan.description: Combine what you've learned to tell a more complete story about your background, explaining that you are from one place but now live in another.
objectives: Use 'ṣùgbọ́n' (but) to connect two ideas.
Form a sentence that states both origin and current residence.
Understand a similar statement from someone else.
plan.conversationGoal: I can explain that I am from one place but now live in a different place.
situations: Giving a more detailed introduction to a new friend or host family.
Explaining your presence in Nigeria as a foreigner.
Sharing your background story in a small group setting.
sentenceGoals: Ọmọ Amẹ́ríkà ni mí, ṣùgbọ́n mo ń gbé ní Èkó. -> I am an American, but I live in Lagos.
Mo wá láti abúlé kan, ṣùgbọ́n mo ń gbé ní ìlú ńlá nísinsìnyí. -> I came from a village, but I live in the big city now.
Ó wá láti Ìbàdàn, ṣùgbọ́n ó ń gbé ní Abuja. -> He/She is from Ibadan, but he/she lives in Abuja.
plan.focusSummary: This lesson synthesizes the unit's concepts by teaching how to use 'ṣùgbọ́n' (but) to connect statements about origin and residence, creating more complex and descriptive sentences.

plan.title: Asking 'Where Are You From?'
plan.description: Learn the fundamental question for asking about someone's origin and how to give a simple answer stating the city or country you come from.
objectives: Ask 'Where are you from?' in Yoruba.
State where you are from using 'Mo wá láti...'
Recognize and name a few countries and major Nigerian cities in Yoruba.
plan.conversationGoal: Ask someone where they are from and tell them where you are from.

Introducing yourself in a classroom or group setting.
Making small talk with a vendor you see regularly.
plan.focusSummary: This lesson introduces the core question 'Níbo lo ti wá?' (Where are you from?) and the simple answer structure 'Mo wá láti...' (I am from...), focusing on names of places.

plan.title: Stating Your Nationality
plan.description: Learn a common and natural way to state your nationality or origin using the 'ọmọ' construction, which means 'native of' or 'child of' a place.
objectives: Understand and use the 'ọmọ' construction to state nationality.
Ask about someone's nationality.
Identify yourself as being from a specific country or ethnic group.
plan.conversationGoal: Ask someone their nationality and state your own using a culturally common expression.
situations: Clarifying your background when someone asks where you're from.
Answering questions for a registration form.
Introducing a friend and mentioning their origin.
plan.focusSummary: This lesson focuses on expressing nationality with the 'ọmọ... ni...' structure (e.g., 'Ọmọ Nàìjíríà ni mí'), a key cultural and linguistic concept for identity.

plan.title: Asking 'Where Do You Live?'
plan.description: Distinguish between where you are from and where you currently live. Learn to ask and answer questions about your place of residence.
objectives: Ask 'Where do you live?' using 'ń gbé'.
State where you live using 'Mo ń gbé ní...'
Use the continuous tense marker 'ń' with the verb 'gbé' (to live).
plan.conversationGoal: Ask where someone currently lives and tell them where you reside.
situations: Making plans with a new friend and needing to know their location.
Small talk with a new colleague about your neighborhoods.
Getting to know a neighbor better.
plan.focusSummary: This lesson introduces the concept of current residence with the question 'Níbo lo ń gbé?' (Where do you live?) and the answer 'Mo ń gbé ní...' (I live in...).

plan.title: Combining Origin and Residence
plan.description: Practice combining information about your origin and your current residence in a single conversation, using the conjunction 'ṣùgbọ́n' (but).
objectives: Review asking about origin and residence.
Connect two ideas using 'ṣùgbọ́n' (but).
Form a more detailed sentence describing your background.
plan.conversationGoal: Explain that you are from one place but now live in another.
situations: Giving a more detailed introduction to a new friend.
Explaining your presence in Nigeria as a foreigner.
Talking about family background and current living situation.
sentenceGoals: I am from Ibadan, but I live in Lagos now.
He is an American, but he lives here.
My parents are from the village, but we live in the city.
plan.focusSummary: This lesson integrates previous concepts, teaching learners to combine origin and residence information using 'ṣùgbọ́n' (but) to create more nuanced self-introductions.

finalPlan.title: Sunrise and the First Hello
finalPlan.description: Learn the essential Yoruba morning greetings, focusing on how to show respect to elders and how to greet friends casually.
objectives: Identify the correct morning greeting for the time of day.
Distinguish between the respectful and casual forms of good morning.
Apply the honorific pronoun when greeting an older family member.
finalPlan.conversationGoal: Greet family members appropriately in the morning, choosing the respectful form for elders and the casual form for peers.
situations: Walking into the kitchen and greeting an older aunt.
Saying good morning to a sibling or friend.
finalPlan.focusSummary: Morning greetings with a focus on the respectful 'Ẹ káàárọ̀' versus the casual 'Káàárọ̀'.

finalPlan.title: High Sun and the Afternoon Hustle
finalPlan.description: Navigate afternoon interactions in public spaces by using the correct respectful and casual daytime greetings.
objectives: Recognize the standard afternoon greeting used between 12 PM and 4 PM.
Use the honorific form for strangers, bosses, and elders.
Switch to the casual form when addressing younger siblings or close peers.
finalPlan.conversationGoal: Confidently greet strangers and market vendors in the afternoon heat using the respectful Yoruba honorific.
situations: Approaching a stall owner at a market in the heat of the day.
Greeting a younger sibling when returning from an errand.
finalPlan.focusSummary: Afternoon greetings focusing on 'Ẹ káàsán' and 'Káàsán' for public and professional settings.

finalPlan.title: The Cooling Evening
finalPlan.description: Master the early evening transition greetings used between 4 PM and 7 PM as the day begins to cool down.
objectives: Learn the specific early evening greeting.
Recognize and practice the vowel contraction required for fluent pronunciation.
Demonstrate respect to older neighbors sitting outside.
finalPlan.conversationGoal: Greet older neighbors respectfully in the early evening as you return home from work or school.
situations: Greeting an older neighbor who is sitting outside their house.
Exchanging casual evening greetings with a peer.
sentenceGoals: Good early evening to you, elder.
finalPlan.focusSummary: Early evening greetings highlighting the vowel contraction in 'Ẹ kúùrọ̀lẹ́' and 'Kúùrọ̀lẹ́'.

finalPlan.title: The Nighttime Greeting
finalPlan.description: Conclude the 24-hour respect loop by learning the proper greetings used from 7 PM onwards.
objectives: Identify the correct nighttime greeting.
Apply the plural/honorific form when addressing a group or an elder.
Understand when the daytime greetings transition into night.
finalPlan.conversationGoal: Show respect to a group of elders or family members when encountering them late at night.
situations: Greeting a group of elders or family members encountered late at night.
Saying good evening casually to a friend before parting ways.
sentenceGoals: May we wake up well tomorrow.
finalPlan.focusSummary: Nighttime greetings from 7 PM onwards, focusing on the respectful 'Ẹ káalẹ́' and casual 'Káalẹ́'.
